topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The campus map, once a vibrant tapestry of pathways and landmarks, now felt like a cruel joke. Every time I traced the route from the library to the student union, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I kept catching glimpses of the 'For Sale' signs plastered near the administration building, their stark white lettering mocking the familiar brickwork. My classmates, laughing easily in the quad, seemed oblivious, their easy camaraderie a sharp contrast to the sudden, heavy silence that settled over my own movements. I smoothed down my worn sweater repeatedly, a nervous habit that felt entirely unnecessary, as if I were trying to smooth out the very edges of my presence. When Professor Davies mentioned the restructuring in his lecture, I gripped my pen so tightly my knuckles turned white, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I found myself avoiding the main thoroughfares. The usual buzz of hundreds of students—the hurried footsteps, the overlapping conversations—now sounded amplified, each sound highlighting my own isolation. Walking past the old campus bookstore, where I used to spend hours browsing, I felt a distinct urge to shrink, to become invisible. My backpack felt impossibly heavy, weighted down by the knowledge that this place, the place I had built a routine around, was dissolving. I kept my gaze fixed on the pavement, meticulously cataloging the cracks, anything to avoid meeting anyone’s eyes. A casual greeting from a friend felt like a spotlight, illuminating every insecurity I carried about being just another temporary fixture on this shrinking landscape.

[story 3]

The official notice sat on my desk, crisp and unforgiving, the ink seeming to vibrate under the fluorescent lights. I stared at the final date, a date that felt less like a deadline and more like an eviction notice from my entire existence here. I picked up a forgotten notebook, flipping through pages filled with half-formed ideas, and a wave of profound awkwardness washed over me. How was I supposed to transition? How would I explain to people that this institution, the backdrop to so many of my formative memories, was simply… ending? I fiddled with the frayed edge of the paper, my breath catching in my throat. Every casual glance from a passing acquaintance felt like an assessment, a silent judgment about my ability to cope with this sudden, massive shift in reality.
